methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Nuclear Energy (found 1 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 4 same-sector peers exist in Nuclear Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
General Matter, a Series A nuclear energy company founded by Scott Nolan and Lee Robinson, is evaluated against a sector universe of 3 public peers (OKLO, NNE, SMR) and 8 private competitors including Fuse Energy, X-Energy, and Standard Nuclear. The company has raised $50M+ to date.

Stage-progression analysis applies a 2.0x multiplier to the $50M total-raised proxy, yielding an implied valuation of $100M. This method is appropriate for Series A positioning and reflects typical capital step-ups between financing rounds. Peer-median analysis is inapplicable due to insufficient same-stage and adjacent-stage comparables within the narrow nuclear energy segment—while 4 same-sector peers exist, fewer than 3 datapoints cluster at matching stage or valuation tier, making sector-wide benchmarking unreliable.

The triangulation supports a valuation range of $85M to $115M, with a median of $100M. This single-method range reflects reliance on stage-progression mechanics rather than market-based peer validation. Key uncertainties include the absence of tight peer comparables in early-stage nuclear tech, limiting confidence in relative valuation, and the quality and composition of the $50M raised (seed vs. Series A mix), which affects the validity of the proxy base. A peer set expansion or disclosure of recent transaction multiples in the sector would meaningfully narrow this band.
